Scenario C: Service 6 years, balance Rs.2,00,000 → NO TDS (service >= 5 years). Scenario D: Service 3 years, no PAN → TDS = 20% on amount above threshold. Case: Bhavna Bhatt v. Regional Provident Fund Commissioner (Delhi High Court, 2018). Relevance: Governs death claim process when no valid nomination exists. FACTS: Member died without having filed Form 2 nomination. Widow approached EPFO for PF settlement. EPFO demanded succession certificate from civil court, causing delay. HELD: Legal heir certificate from competent authority (Tehsildar / Revenue Officer / SDM) or court affidavit is sufficient for PF claim settlement when no nomination exists. EPFO cannot insist on succession certificate from civil court in all cases — this causes undue hardship to families. PROCESS FOR DEATH CLAIM WITHOUT NOMINATION: 1. Widow/widower can claim using legal heir certificate from SDM/Tehsildar 2. Children can claim with their birth certificates + death certificate of member 3. EPFO processes under Para 70(ii) of EPF Scheme — equal shares among family members 4. EPS family pension: Automatically payable to spouse. Submit Form 10D. 5. EDLI benefit: Form 5-IF. Same documents. DOCUMENTS NEEDED FOR DEATH CLAIM: - Death certificate of member - Legal heir certificate (SDM/Tehsildar) OR succession certificate from court if no legal heir cert available - Bank account details of claimant - Aadhaar of claimant (for UAN-linked accounts) - Age proof of widow/children - Form 20 (EPF), Form 10D (EPS pension), Form 5-IF (EDLI)

Practical guide on TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) for PF withdrawals, reflecting rules effective from April 2022 and updated in 2024. THE SIMPLE RULE — TWO CONDITIONS MUST BOTH BE TRUE FOR TDS: Condition 1: Total service is LESS than 5 years (counting service across all employers if PF was transferred) AND Condition 2: Withdrawal amount is MORE than Rs.50,000 If EITHER condition is false → NO TDS is deducted. COUNTING THE 5 YEARS: - Service includes all employers where PF was contributed AND transferred to your current account - Example: 3 years at Company A (transferred) + 2.5 years at Company B = 5.5 years total → NO TDS - If you did NOT transfer PF from previous employer, each account is treated separately - TDS applies per withdrawal claim, not per employer TDS RATES (for residential Indians with PAN): Scenario | TDS Rate PAN valid + service < 5 yrs + > 50k | 10% No valid PAN + service < 5 yrs + >50k | 20% Service 5+ years (regardless of amount)| 0% Amount ≤ Rs.50,000 | 0% HOW TO SUBMIT FORM 15G (to get zero TDS): WHO: Resident individual below 60 years whose estimated total income for the year is below Rs.2,50,000 (i.e., below basic exemption limit). WHEN: Submit BEFORE filing the withdrawal claim. Cannot be submitted after TDS is deducted. WHERE (Online): Login to member portal > Online Services > Claim > You'll see a checkbox/link to upload Form 15G before submitting the claim. Upload 2 copies. WHERE (Offline): Submit 2 physical copies along with claim form to PF office.

An EPF account is classified as Inoperative when no contribution has been received for 3 years after:
        - Retirement
        - Permanent migration abroad
        - Death
        
        INTEREST ON INOPERATIVE ACCOUNTS:
        - At present, all accounts earn interest up to age 58 of the member
        - After account becomes inoperative, no further interest is credited
        - Account becomes inoperative after 36 months from date of retirement (55 years+) or permanent migration
        
        WHAT TO DO WITH INOPERATIVE ACCOUNT:
        - If still employed in covered establishment: Get amount transferred to new account (online or offline Form 13)
        - If retired: Withdraw the amount by submitting withdrawal claim
        
        UNCLAIMED PF (SENIOR CITIZENS FUND):
        Unclaimed EPF amounts are transferred to Senior Citizens Welfare Fund (SCWF) after 7 years of becoming inoperative. However, members can still claim this amount from EPFO.
        
        HOW TO CLAIM INOPERATIVE ACCOUNT:
        - Trace the account using establishment name or PF number
        - Contact the employer for details
        - Submit withdrawal claim to concerned Regional PF Office with proper KYC documentation
        
        PASSBOOK FOR INOPERATIVE ACCOUNTS:
        - For exempted establishments: Passbook not visible on UAN portal; contact establishment directly
        - For non-exempted: Login to UAN portal passbook section

(basic exemption for senior citizens). 2024 UPDATES — KEY CHANGES: 1. Non-resident (NRI) members: TDS at 30% (or DTAA rate if lower) — Form 15G/15H not available to NRIs. File Form 10F for DTAA benefit. WHAT HAPPENS AFTER TDS DEDUCTION: - Form 16A (TDS certificate) is issued by EPFO within 15 days from due date of TDS return - TDS can be claimed as credit in your Income Tax Return (ITR) - If your total income is below taxable limit, you can claim TDS refund from Income Tax Department EXAMPLE SCENARIOS: Scenario A: Service 4 years, balance Rs.80,000, valid PAN → TDS = 10% of Rs.80,000 = Rs.8,000. EPFO introduced auto-transfer feature for UAN-seeded members changing jobs. Previous account must have a balance HOW TO CHECK IF AUTO-TRANSFER HAPPENED: Login to member portal > View > Transfer Passbook / Passbook. If a large credit appears shortly after joining new employer, it's the auto-transfer. OR: Online Services > Track Claim Status > look for a settled transfer claim. IF AUTO-TRANSFER DID NOT TRIGGER: File manual Form 13 transfer request via Online Services > One Member One EPF Account. Common reasons auto-transfer fails: Date of exit not updated, previous establishment is exempted, Aadhaar not seeded, UIDAI name mismatch. MULTIPLE OLD ACCOUNTS: If you have 3+ old accounts, only the most recent eligible one may auto-transfer. File manual transfer requests for older accounts separately.

Practical guide on TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) for PF withdrawals, reflecting rules effective from April 2022 and updated in 2024. THE SIMPLE RULE — TWO CONDITIONS MUST BOTH BE TRUE FOR TDS: Condition 1: Total service is LESS than 5 years (counting service across all employers if PF was transferred) AND Condition 2: Withdrawal amount is MORE than Rs.50,000 If EITHER condition is false → NO TDS is deducted. COUNTING THE 5 YEARS: - Service includes all employers where PF was contributed AND transferred to your current account - Example: 3 years at Company A (transferred) + 2.5 years at Company B = 5.5 years total → NO TDS - If you did NOT transfer PF from previous employer, each account is treated separately - TDS applies per withdrawal claim, not per employer TDS RATES (for residential Indians with PAN): Scenario | TDS Rate PAN valid + service < 5 yrs + > 50k | 10% No valid PAN + service < 5 yrs + >50k | 20% Service 5+ years (regardless of amount)| 0% Amount ≤ Rs.50,000 | 0% HOW TO SUBMIT FORM 15G (to get zero TDS): WHO: Resident individual below 60 years whose estimated total income for the year is below Rs.2,50,000 (i.e., below basic exemption limit). WHEN: Submit BEFORE filing the withdrawal claim. Cannot be submitted after TDS is deducted. WHERE (Online): Login to member portal > Online Services > Claim > You'll see a checkbox/link to upload Form 15G before submitting the claim. Upload 2 copies. Case: Bhavna Bhatt v. Regional Provident Fund Commissioner (Delhi High Court, 2018). Relevance: Governs death claim process when no valid nomination exists. FACTS: Member died without having filed Form 2 nomination. Widow approached EPFO for PF settlement. EPFO demanded succession certificate from civil court, causing delay. HELD: Legal heir certificate from competent authority (Tehsildar / Revenue Officer / SDM) or court affidavit is sufficient for PF claim settlement when no nomination exists. EPFO cannot insist on succession certificate from civil court in all cases — this causes undue hardship to families. PROCESS FOR DEATH CLAIM WITHOUT NOMINATION: 1. Widow/widower can claim using legal heir certificate from SDM/Tehsildar 2. Children can claim with their birth certificates + death certificate of member 3. EPFO processes under Para 70(ii) of EPF Scheme — equal shares among family members 4. EPS family pension: Automatically payable to spouse. Submit Form 10D. 5. EDLI benefit: Form 5-IF. Same documents. DOCUMENTS NEEDED FOR DEATH CLAIM: - Death certificate of member - Legal heir certificate (SDM/Tehsildar) OR succession certificate from court if no legal heir cert available - Bank account details of claimant - Aadhaar of claimant (for UAN-linked accounts) - Age proof of widow/children - Form 20 (EPF), Form 10D (EPS pension), Form 5-IF (EDLI)

Practical step-by-step guide to transfer your PF when you change jobs. Transfer is always recommended over withdrawal to preserve service history, compounding interest, and pension benefit. WHY TRANSFER INSTEAD OF WITHDRAW: 1. Service continuity: Total service ≥ 5 years → no TDS on future withdrawal 2. Service continuity: Total service ≥ 10 years → eligible for EPS pension 3. Interest compounding: PF earns 8.25% (2023-24), doubles roughly every 8-9 years 4. No tax: Transfer is not a withdrawal — zero tax on transfer 5. Pension history: EPS service record carries over even though pension amount itself is not transferred WHEN TO INITIATE TRANSFER: Best time: After joining new employer and first contribution appears in new PF account (typically after 45 days of joining). You need: New PF Member ID OR new UAN-linked account to be active. PREREQUISITES (CHECKLIST): □ Old employer has marked your Date of Exit in EPFO system → If not: You can self-update after 60 days of leaving (Manage > Mark Exit in member portal) □ New employer has generated your UAN/Member ID and made at least 1 contribution □ Your UAN is active and has Aadhaar + bank account linked (KYC complete) □ Old employer had approved KYC (Aadhaar / bank) — needed for online transfer eligibility STEP-BY-STEP ONLINE TRANSFER (Form 13): Step 1: Login to https://unifiedportal-mem.epfindia.gov.in > Online Services > One Member - One EPF Account (Transfer Request). Step 2: Verify personal details and click 'Request for Transfe

[Source 2] doc_id=NEWS_AUTO_TRANSFER_UAN_2023 | date=2023-01-01 | domain=pf
EPFO introduced auto-transfer feature for UAN-seeded members changing jobs. HOW IT WORKS: When an employee with Aadhaar-seeded UAN joins a new employer and the first EPF contribution is received in the new Member ID, the system checks if the same UAN has other active Member IDs with balance. If yes, a transfer request is automatically generated. CONDITIONS FOR AUTO-TRANSFER: 1. UAN must be Aadhaar-seeded (UIDAI verified) 2. Date of exit from previous employer must be updated (either by employer or self-marked after 60 days) 3. New employer must have made at least 1 contribution in the new Member ID 4. Both previous and new establishments must be unexempted (EPFO-maintained, not private trust) 5. Previous account must have a balance HOW TO CHECK IF AUTO-TRANSFER HAPPENED: Login to member portal > View > Transfer Passbook / Passbook. If a large credit appears shortly after joining new employer, it's the auto-transfer. OR: Online Services > Track Claim Status > look for a settled transfer claim. IF AUTO-TRANSFER DID NOT TRIGGER: File manual Form 13 transfer request via Online Services > One Member One EPF Account. Common reasons auto-transfer fails: Date of exit not updated, previous establishment is exempted, Aadhaar not seeded, UIDAI name mismatch. MULTIPLE OLD ACCOUNTS: If you have 3+ old accounts, only the most recent eligible one may auto-transfer. File manual transfer requests for older accounts separately.

Complete reference for EPFO forms and their uses.

WITHDRAWAL / SETTLEMENT FORMS:
Form 19: PF Final Settlement (Full withdrawal). Online if KYC complete.
Form 10C: Scheme Certificate (retain pension membership) OR Withdrawal Benefit (if service < 10 years). Online if KYC complete.
Form 10D: Monthly Pension claim. For members/family seeking EPS pension.
Form 31: Non-refundable Advance / Partial withdrawal. Online if KYC complete.
Form 20: Death case — PF withdrawal by nominee/legal heir.
Form 5-IF: EDLI (Insurance) benefit claim by nominee/legal heir.
Composite Claim Form (Aadhaar): Replaces Forms 19, 10C, 31 — can be submitted without employer attestation. Online and offline.
Composite Claim Form (Non-Aadhaar): Replaces Forms 19, 10C, 31 — requires employer attestation. Offline only.
Another Composite Form: Replaces Forms 20, 10D, 5-IF for death/pension cases.

TRANSFER FORMS:
Form 13 / Form 13(R): PF transfer request from previous to current account. Online preferred; physical only for exempted-to-exempted.
Annexure-K: Transfer from exempted trust — details sent by trust to EPFO/receiving trust.

MEMBER REGISTRATION / KYC:
Form 11 (New): Declaration of previous EPF service on new joining. Enables auto-transfer.
Form 2: Nomination form for EPF, EPS, and EDLI. Family and nominee details.
Form 3A/ECR Copy: Contribution card. Member can demand from employer.

TAX FORMS:
Form 15G: Declaration for zero TDS if total income below Rs.2.5 lakh (non-senior citizens, any age). Needs PAN

Withdrew my PF of ₹250000. Service was 6 years. Will TDS apply?

DOMAIN: pf

[Source 1] doc_id=CASE_BHAVNA_BHATT_DELHI_HC_2018 | date=2018-01-01 | domain=pf
Case: Bhavna Bhatt v. Regional Provident Fund Commissioner (Delhi High Court, 2018). Relevance: Governs death claim process when no valid nomination exists. FACTS: Member died without having filed Form 2 nomination. Widow approached EPFO for PF settlement. EPFO demanded succession certificate from civil court, causing delay. HELD: Legal heir certificate from competent authority (Tehsildar / Revenue Officer / SDM) or court affidavit is sufficient for PF claim settlement when no nomination exists. EPFO cannot insist on succession certificate from civil court in all cases — this causes undue hardship to families. PROCESS FOR DEATH CLAIM WITHOUT NOMINATION: 1. Widow/widower can claim using legal heir certificate from SDM/Tehsildar 2. Children can claim with their birth certificates + death certificate of member 3. EPFO processes under Para 70(ii) of EPF Scheme — equal shares among family members 4. EPS family pension: Automatically payable to spouse. Submit Form 10D. 5. EDLI benefit: Form 5-IF. Same documents. DOCUMENTS NEEDED FOR DEATH CLAIM: - Death certificate of member - Legal heir certificate (SDM/Tehsildar) OR succession certificate from court if no legal heir cert available - Bank account details of claimant - Aadhaar of claimant (for UAN-linked accounts) - Age proof of widow/children - Form 20 (EPF), Form 10D (EPS pension), Form 5-IF (EDLI)

[Source 1] doc_id=RO_RO_LUCKNOW | date=2024-01-01 | domain=pf
EPFO Regional Office Lucknow: Bhavishya Nidhi Bhavan, 10 Ashok Marg, Lucknow 226001. Phone: 0522-2238858. Email: ro.lucknow@epfindia.gov.in. Covers: Uttar Pradesh (East). SROs: Varanasi, Allahabad, Gorakhpur.

[Source 2] doc_id=CASE_CONTRACTOR_EPFO_LIABILITY_SC_2020 | date=2020-01-01 | domain=pf
Consolidated principle from multiple Supreme Court judgments including EPFO v. Fancy Weaving Works (2020) and related contractor liability cases. LEGAL POSITION: Under Section 8A of EPF & MP Act 1952, the Principal Employer is responsible for ensuring EPF compliance for ALL employees working on the premises, including those employed through contractors. KEY RULES: 1. If contractor fails to enroll employees or deposit PF, PRINCIPAL EMPLOYER IS LIABLE. 2. Principal employer cannot escape liability by claiming the contractor was independent. 3. Principal employer CAN recover the EPF amount paid from the contractor. 4. Workers cannot be denied EPF simply because contractor is unregistered. RPFC has powers to recover from principal employer. Bring: salary slip, ID card, any communication showing employment at the establishment. Complete practical guide to filing a grievance on EPFiGMS (EPF i-Grievance Management System) at https://epfigms.gov.in. WHEN TO FILE A GRIEVANCE: - PF withdrawal claim not settled within 20 working days - KYC not approved by employer despite multiple requests (wait at least 7 days first) - Employer not depositing PF contributions - Transfer claim stuck or not processed - Claim rejected for reasons you disagree with or cannot resolve - Incorrect PF balance shown - UAN issues not resolved by normal means - Passbook not updated for more than 2 months after contribution STEP-BY-STEP GRIEVANCE FILING: Step 1: Go to https://epfigms.gov.in Step 2: Click 'Register Grievance'. Step 3: Select your type: → 'EPF Members/Pensioners' (for current/former employees) → 'Employer' (for establishments) → 'Others' (if not enrolled / UAN not available) Step 4: Enter your UAN (preferred) OR PF Member ID OR PPO Number (for pensioners). [Source 1] doc_id=NEWS_AUTO_TRANSFER_UAN_2023 | date=2023-01-01 | domain=pf
EPFO introduced auto-transfer feature for UAN-seeded members changing jobs. HOW IT WORKS: When an employee with Aadhaar-seeded UAN joins a new employer and the first EPF contribution is received in the new Member ID, the system checks if the same UAN has other active Member IDs with balance. If yes, a transfer request is automatically generated. CONDITIONS FOR AUTO-TRANSFER: 1. UAN must be Aadhaar-seeded (UIDAI verified) 2. Date of exit from previous employer must be updated (either by employer or self-marked after 60 days) 3. New employer must have made at least 1 contribution in the new Member ID 4. Both previous and new establishments must be unexempted (EPFO-maintained, not private trust) 5. Previous account must have a balance HOW TO CHECK IF AUTO-TRANSFER HAPPENED: Login to member portal > View > Transfer Passbook / Passbook. If a large credit appears shortly after joining new employer, it's the auto-transfer. OR: Online Services > Track Claim Status > look for a settled transfer claim. IF AUTO-TRANSFER DID NOT TRIGGER: File manual Form 13 transfer request via Online Services > One Member One EPF Account. Common reasons auto-transfer fails: Date of exit not updated, previous establishment is exempted, Aadhaar not seeded, UIDAI name mismatch. MULTIPLE OLD ACCOUNTS: If you have 3+ old accounts, only the most recent eligible one may auto-transfer. File manual transfer requests for older accounts separately.

DOMAIN: pf

[Source 1] doc_id=EMPLOYER_DEFAULT_REMEDIES | date=2024-01-01 | domain=pf
When an employer deducts PF from employee wages but does not deposit with EPFO, there are several remedies available.
        
        HOW TO DETECT DEFAULT:
        - Check monthly contribution in e-passbook (activate UAN at unifiedportal-mem.epfindia.gov.in)
        - Receive SMS on registered mobile when monthly contribution is credited
        - Annual PF Statement of Account shows default periods
        
        REMEDIES FOR EMPLOYEE:
        1. File online grievance at https://epfigms.gov.in with salary slip showing PF deduction
        2. Approach Regional PF Commissioner with documentary evidence
        3. Appear before Commissioner in 'Nidhi Apke Nikat' program (10th of every month)
        
        LEGAL ACTIONS EPFO CAN TAKE AGAINST EMPLOYER:
        - Attachment of Bank Accounts (under Section 8B of EPF & MP Act)
        - Recovery from Debtors of employer
        - Attachment and Sale of employer's properties
        - Arrest and Detention of the Employer
        - Action under Section 406/409 IPC and Section 110 CrPC
        - Prosecution under section 14 of EPF & MP Act, 1952
        - Penal interest under section 7Q
        - Penal damages under section 14B
        
        IMPACT ON MEMBER:
        - PF amount will be paid only to the extent realised from employer
        - Member still receives full interest on amount actually deposited
        - Pension is GUARANTEED even if employer defaults on pension contribution
        - EDLI benefit is payable only

An EPF account is classified as Inoperative when no contribution has been received for 3 years after:
        - Retirement
        - Permanent migration abroad
        - Death
        
        INTEREST ON INOPERATIVE ACCOUNTS:
        - At present, all accounts earn interest up to age 58 of the member
        - After account becomes inoperative, no further interest is credited
        - Account becomes inoperative after 36 months from date of retirement (55 years+) or permanent migration
        
        WHAT TO DO WITH INOPERATIVE ACCOUNT:
        - If still employed in covered establishment: Get amount transferred to new account (online or offline Form 13)
        - If retired: Withdraw the amount by submitting withdrawal claim

The Employees' State Insurance (ESI) scheme applies to employees whose gross salary is ₹21,000 per month or less (₹25,000 for employees with disabilities). The ESI contribution rates are: employee 0.75% of gross wages, employer 3.25% of gross wages. ESI applies to establishments with 10 or more employees (20 in some states). Employees earning above ₹21,000 gross are not covered by ESI.

---

Salary 15,001–20,000: ₹150/month. Salary >20,000: ₹200/month. Employers must deduct PT from each employee's salary and remit to the state government. Failure to deduct or remit PT is an offence under the state PT Act.

USER QUERY:
Worked 1 years. Employer terminated me without notice or any written communication. factory company.

DOMAIN: labour

RETRIEVED PASSAGES:
[Source 1] doc_id=NOTICE_PERIOD_GARDEN_LEAVE | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Garden leave (also called 'gardening leave') is a practice where an employer asks an employee to stay away from the workplace during the notice period while continuing to pay their full salary. This is legally permissible if the employment contract or standing orders allow it. During garden leave, the employee's employment continues — they are still entitled to salary, benefits, and statutory entitlements such as EPF contributions. Garden leave clauses are common in senior roles and IT/financial sector contracts where the employer wants to prevent the employee from immediately joining a competitor. The employee cannot be compelled to work if placed on garden leave.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=STANDING_ORDERS_ACT_NOTICE_PERIOD | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

USER QUERY:
Worked 5 years at a factory company. employer_terminated. Last drawn salary was ₹25000. Am I eligible for gratuity?

DOMAIN: labour

RETRIEVED PASSAGES:
[Source 1] doc_id=GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=GRATUITY_COURT_RULING_4Y8M | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.

ELIGIBILITY REASONING TRACE:
  Decision : ANSWER
  Eligible : True
  Coverage : 1.0
  Met conditions:
    ✓ employment_years gte 5 [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG]
    ✓ termination_reason in ['resignation', 'retirement', 'employer_terminated', 'retrenched'] [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG]

SLOTS FILLED:
{
  "intent": "gratuity",
  "employment_years": 5,
  "termination_reason": "employer_terminated",
  "employer_type": "factory",
  "last_drawn_salary": 25000
}

Produce the final answer now.

**Eligibility for Gratuity:**
You are eligible for gratuity.

**Reason:** You have completed 5 years of continuous service, which is the minimum requirement for gratuity as per Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972 [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG]. Your termination reason is 'employer_terminated', which is one of the eligible reasons for gratuity [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].

**Next Steps:**

1.  Check your last drawn salary, which is ₹25,000. This includes basic wages plus dearness allowance [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].
2.  Calculate your gratuity using the formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. For your case, this would be (25000 × 15 × 5) ÷ 26.
3.  The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. If your calculated gratuity exceeds this amount, you will receive ₹20 Lakhs.

**Warning:** Be aware that your employer must pay gratuity within 30 days of becoming due and will have to pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay [GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG].

The Industrial Relations Code 2020 consolidates the Industrial Disputes Act, the Trade Unions Act, and the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act. Under the Code, establishments with 300 or more workers require prior government permission before retrenchment or closure. Establishments with fewer than 300 workers can retrench workers by following the notice and compensation provisions without seeking prior permission. This threshold was raised from 100 workers under the Industrial Disputes Act.

When an employee resigns and is unable or unwilling to serve the full notice period specified in the employment contract or standing orders, the employer may allow a 'notice buy-out' — the employee pays the employer wages equivalent to the shortfall in notice days. The amount is typically calculated as (Basic + DA) ÷ 30 × remaining notice days. The employer cannot withhold the full and final settlement solely because of a notice period dispute; any recovery must be through civil proceedings or agreed deduction from F&F. An employee cannot be forced to serve the notice period against their will — specific performance of a service contract is barred under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act 1963.

Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

An employer may, at its discretion, waive the notice period and relieve the employee immediately or earlier than the contractual notice end date. In this case, the employer must pay the employee salary for the full notice period (pay in lieu of notice) unless the employee voluntarily agrees to forgo it. If the employer relieves the employee early without notice pay, the employee can recover the shortfall as unpaid wages under the Payment of Wages Act 1936 or through a civil suit. Early relieving by mutual consent is the cleanest route and should be documented in writing.

---

When an employee resigns and is unable or unwilling to serve the full notice period specified in the employment contract or standing orders, the employer may allow a 'notice buy-out' — the employee pays the employer wages equivalent to the shortfall in notice days. The amount is typically calculated as (Basic + DA) ÷ 30 × remaining notice days. The employer cannot withhold the full and final settlement solely because of a notice period dispute; any recovery must be through civil proceedings or agreed deduction from F&F. An employee cannot be forced to serve the notice period against their will — specific performance of a service contract is barred under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act 1963.

Garden leave (also called 'gardening leave') is a practice where an employer asks an employee to stay away from the workplace during the notice period while continuing to pay their full salary. This is legally permissible if the employment contract or standing orders allow it. During garden leave, the employee's employment continues — they are still entitled to salary, benefits, and statutory entitlements such as EPF contributions. Garden leave clauses are common in senior roles and IT/financial sector contracts where the employer wants to prevent the employee from immediately joining a competitor. The employee cannot be compelled to work if placed on garden leave.

'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. DOMAIN: labour

[Source 1] doc_id=STANDING_ORDERS_ACT_NOTICE_PERIOD | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=WRONGFUL_TERMINATION_REMEDIES | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
An employee who has been wrongfully terminated has several remedies depending on their category: (1) Workmen (as defined under the Industrial Disputes Act) can raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Commissioner, and if unresolved, before the Labour Court, which can award reinstatement with back wages. (2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=ID_ACT_S25F_RETRENCHMENT | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 25F of the Industrial Disputes Act 1947, an employer cannot retrench a workman who has completed one year of continuous service without: (a) giving one month's written notice or pay in lieu; (b) paying retrenchment compensation at 15 days' average pay for every completed year of service; and (c) giving notice to the appropriate government authority. Retrenchment without these steps is unlawful. An aggrieved employee can file a complaint before the Labour Court within 3 years of the retrenchment.

The employer cannot withhold the full and final settlement solely because of a notice period dispute; any recovery must be through civil proceedings or agreed deduction from F&F. When an employee resigns and is unable or unwilling to serve the full notice period specified in the employment contract or standing orders, the employer may allow a 'notice buy-out' — the employee pays the employer wages equivalent to the shortfall in notice days. Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

[Source 1] doc_id=WRONGFUL_TERMINATION_REMEDIES | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
An employee who has been wrongfully terminated has several remedies depending on their category: (1) Workmen (as defined under the Industrial Disputes Act) can raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Commissioner, and if unresolved, before the Labour Court, which can award reinstatement with back wages. (2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=CODE_IR_2020_RETRENCHMENT | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Industrial Relations Code 2020 consolidates the Industrial Disputes Act, the Trade Unions Act, and the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act. Under the Code, establishments with 300 or more workers require prior government permission before retrenchment or closure. Establishments with fewer than 300 workers can retrench workers by following the notice and compensation provisions without seeking prior permission. This threshold was raised from 100 workers under the Industrial Disputes Act.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=RELIEVING_LETTER_LEGAL_OBLIGATION | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
There is no specific central statute mandating that an employer must issue a relieving letter. However, withholding a relieving letter as coercion or to prevent future employment may be challenged as restraint of trade under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act 1872. An employee can approach the Labour Commissioner or file a civil suit for recovery of the relieving letter. Several High Courts have directed employers to issue relieving letters when withheld without valid cause.

The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=GRATUITY_ACT_S4_ELIG | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

DOMAIN: labour

[Source 1] doc_id=CODE_IR_2020_RETRENCHMENT | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Industrial Relations Code 2020 consolidates the Industrial Disputes Act, the Trade Unions Act, and the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act. Under the Code, establishments with 300 or more workers require prior government permission before retrenchment or closure. Establishments with fewer than 300 workers can retrench workers by following the notice and compensation provisions without seeking prior permission. This threshold was raised from 100 workers under the Industrial Disputes Act.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=WRONGFUL_TERMINATION_REMEDIES | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
An employee who has been wrongfully terminated has several remedies depending on their category: (1) Workmen (as defined under the Industrial Disputes Act) can raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Commissioner, and if unresolved, before the Labour Court, which can award reinstatement with back wages. (2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

---

Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

Garden leave (also called 'gardening leave') is a practice where an employer asks an employee to stay away from the workplace during the notice period while continuing to pay their full salary. This is legally permissible if the employment contract or standing orders allow it. During garden leave, the employee's employment continues — they are still entitled to salary, benefits, and statutory entitlements such as EPF contributions. Garden leave clauses are common in senior roles and IT/financial sector contracts where the employer wants to prevent the employee from immediately joining a competitor. The employee cannot be compelled to work if placed on garden leave.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=ID_ACT_S25G_LAST_IN_FIRST_OUT | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Section 25G of the Industrial Disputes Act 1947 requires that an employer retrenching workmen in any category must retrench the person most recently employed in that category first (last-in-first-out principle), unless there are valid reasons for departing from this order. Deviation from this rule without reasons constitutes unlawful discrimination in retrenchment.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=WRONGFUL_TERMINATION_REMEDIES | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
An employee who has been wrongfully terminated has several remedies depending on their category: (1) Workmen (as defined under the Industrial Disputes Act) can raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Commissioner, and if unresolved, before the Labour Court, which can award reinstatement with back wages. (2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

The Industrial Relations Code 2020 consolidates the Industrial Disputes Act, the Trade Unions Act, and the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act. Under the Code, establishments with 300 or more workers require prior government permission before retrenchment or closure. Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=NOTICE_PERIOD_WAIVER_BY_EMPLOYER | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
An employer may, at its discretion, waive the notice period and relieve the employee immediately or earlier than the contractual notice end date. In this case, the employer must pay the employee salary for the full notice period (pay in lieu of notice) unless the employee voluntarily agrees to forgo it. If the employer relieves the employee early without notice pay, the employee can recover the shortfall as unpaid wages under the Payment of Wages Act 1936 or through a civil suit. Early relieving by mutual consent is the cleanest route and should be documented in writing.

(2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=NOTICE_PERIOD_BUYOUT_RULE | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
When an employee resigns and is unable or unwilling to serve the full notice period specified in the employment contract or standing orders, the employer may allow a 'notice buy-out' — the employee pays the employer wages equivalent to the shortfall in notice days. The amount is typically calculated as (Basic + DA) ÷ 30 × remaining notice days. The employer cannot withhold the full and final settlement solely because of a notice period dispute; any recovery must be through civil proceedings or agreed deduction from F&F. An employee cannot be forced to serve the notice period against their will — specific performance of a service contract is barred under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act 1963.

[Source 1] doc_id=STANDING_ORDERS_ACT_NOTICE_PERIOD | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=NOTICE_PERIOD_GARDEN_LEAVE | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Garden leave (also called 'gardening leave') is a practice where an employer asks an employee to stay away from the workplace during the notice period while continuing to pay their full salary. This is legally permissible if the employment contract or standing orders allow it. During garden leave, the employee's employment continues — they are still entitled to salary, benefits, and statutory entitlements such as EPF contributions. Garden leave clauses are common in senior roles and IT/financial sector contracts where the employer wants to prevent the employee from immediately joining a competitor. The employee cannot be compelled to work if placed on garden leave.

---

[Source 3] doc_id=ID_ACT_S25G_LAST_IN_FIRST_OUT | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Section 25G of the Industrial Disputes Act 1947 requires that an employer retrenching workmen in any category must retrench the person most recently employed in that category first (last-in-first-out principle), unless there are valid reasons for departing from this order. Deviation from this rule without reasons constitutes unlawful discrimination in retrenchment.

[Source 1] doc_id=GRATUITY_COURT_RULING_4Y8M | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

[Source 1] doc_id=TERMINATION_MISCONDUCT_GRATUITY | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=GRATUITY_COURT_RULING_4Y8M | date=2024-01-01 | domain=labour
The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.

Additionally, the employee's termination reason is "resignation", which is one of the accepted reasons for gratuity entitlement.

**Next Steps:**

1.  Calculate the gratuity amount using the formula: Gratuity = (Last drawn salary × 15/4 × number of years of service) [GRATUITY_COURT_RULING_4Y8M]. For this case, Gratuity = (25000 × 15/4 × 15) = 28125.
2.  Verify the employer's records to ensure they have followed the required disciplinary inquiry and charge sheet process before terminating the employee's services.

**Warning/Caveat:** The employee's gratuity may be forfeited if their services were terminated due to willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property, or any other act of violence. If an income tax refund is not credited within 45 days of ITR processing, the taxpayer can escalate through these steps: (1) Check AIS/26AS to confirm TDS credit is correctly reflected. (2) Raise a refund re-issue request on the e-filing portal under 'Services > Refund Reissue' if the bank account was not pre-validated or account details were wrong. To check refund status: visit incometax.gov.in > Login > e-File > Income Tax Returns > View Filed Returns, or use the NSDL portal at tin.tin.nsdl.com. Common reasons for refund failure: bank account not validated/pre-validated on the IT portal, bank account IFSC changed or closed, PAN-bank account mismatch. To resolve: pre-validate the bank account on the IT portal under 'My Profile > Bank Account'. Refunds are typically credited within 20–45 days of ITR processing.

Common reasons for refund failure: bank account not validated/pre-validated on the IT portal, bank account IFSC changed or closed, PAN-bank account mismatch. (2) Raise a refund re-issue request on the e-filing portal under 'Services > Refund Reissue' if the bank account was not pre-validated or account details were wrong. (3) File a grievance on the e-filing portal under 'e-Nivaran / Grievance' with the processing reference number. (4) For ITRs processed by CPC Bengaluru, use the CPC helpline at 1800-103-0025. Refund interest under Section 244A accrues at 0.5% per month from April 1 of the assessment year (or from the date of filing if filed after April 1) until the date the refund is granted.

Under Section 245 of the Income Tax Act, the Income Tax Department can adjust an income tax refund against any outstanding tax demand from prior assessment years. Before making such an adjustment, the department must serve a notice to the taxpayer. If the taxpayer disputes the outstanding demand, they must respond to the notice within 30 days. Steps to resolve: (1) Log in to the e-filing portal > Pending Actions > Response to Outstanding Demand — submit a response agreeing or disagreeing with the demand. (2) If the demand is incorrect, upload documentary evidence. (3) If the demand arises from a previous ITR, verify via 26AS whether TDS was correctly credited for that year. Failure to respond to the 245 notice results in the refund being automatically adjusted.

USER QUERY:

DOMAIN: tax

[Source 1] doc_id=ITR_REFUND_DELAY_GRIEVANCE | date=2024-01-01 | domain=tax
If an income tax refund is not credited within 45 days of ITR processing, the taxpayer can escalate through these steps: (1) Check AIS/26AS to confirm TDS credit is correctly reflected. (2) Raise a refund re-issue request on the e-filing portal under 'Services > Refund Reissue' if the bank account was not pre-validated or account details were wrong. (3) File a grievance on the e-filing portal under 'e-Nivaran / Grievance' with the processing reference number. (4) For ITRs processed by CPC Bengaluru, use the CPC helpline at 1800-103-0025. Refund interest under Section 244A accrues at 0.5% per month from April 1 of the assessment year (or from the date of filing if filed after April 1) until the date the refund is granted.

---

[Source 2] doc_id=ITR_REFUND_STATUS | date=2024-01-01 | domain=tax
Income tax refunds are issued after ITR processing by the Centralised Processing Centre (CPC) in Bengaluru. To check refund status: visit incometax.gov.in > Login > e-File > Income Tax Returns > View Filed Returns, or use the NSDL portal at tin.tin.nsdl.com. Common reasons for refund failure: bank account not validated/pre-validated on the IT portal, bank account IFSC changed or closed, PAN-bank account mismatch. To resolve: pre-validate the bank account on the IT portal under 'My Profile > Bank Account'. Refunds are typically credited within 20–45 days of ITR processing.

Under Section 245 of the Income Tax Act, the Income Tax Department can adjust an income tax refund against any outstanding tax demand from prior assessment years.
